Q1: I tried to create a structured routine, blocking out about four hours most days for studying across my seventeen courses. I'd usually start with reading materials and making detailed notes since that's how I learn best. Honestly though, with so many courses, my routine often felt more like damage control than actual organized studying. I kept a planner and tried to prioritize, but some weeks everything just piled up and I had to scramble.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I aimed for consistency, studying in the mornings when my mind was clearer, but deadlines would completely throw that off. When multiple assignments overlapped, I'd end up studying at random hours, sometimes late at night when I was already exhausted. The stress of juggling everything made it hard to stick to any real schedule.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or assignment deadlines that got me to log in. I'd also go in when I needed to access specific readings or submit something. Sometimes I logged in just to see what I was behind on, which honestly just made me more anxious. The fear of missing something important kept me checking regularly, even when I didn't have time to actually engage deeply.
Q4: It became routine around the third week when I realized I couldn't keep track of everything without checking daily. I started logging in every morning with my coffee, just scanning through updates. It wasn't enjoyable, more like a survival mechanism. The peer support group I joined encouraged us to stay on top of things, which helped normalize making it part of my day.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful because I could download them and annotate at my own pace. I also found the discussion forums helpful when other students explained concepts in simpler terms. The peer interactions, even though I only posted a few times, gave me reassurance that others were struggling too. That connection made the material feel less overwhelming.
Q6: I often skipped the video lectures or only skimmed them because they took so much time. With nearly three hours of video content to watch, I just couldn't fit it all in alongside everything else. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them but rarely did. Some supplementary resources also felt redundant when I'd already understood the concept from reading.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be assessed or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was stuck on. I needed to see immediate relevance, otherwise the anxiety of falling behind in other areas would take over. If something felt like busywork, I couldn't justify spending time on it when I was already stretched so thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional ones made me feel guilty because I knew they could help but I just didn't have the bandwidth. Sometimes I'd look at optional resources and feel scared that I was missing out on important learning, but then I'd have to prioritize survival over enrichment.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them with handwritten notes at my desk or in the library. I used external YouTube videos to clarify difficult concepts and borrowed textbooks from the library. The LMS was more of a portal to get materials rather than where I actually learned.
Q10: There were many evenings where I spent hours reviewing my printed notes, rewriting key concepts, and testing myself with flashcards I'd made. None of that showed up anywhere online. I also had study sessions with peers from my support group where we'd work through problems together in person. Those were some of my most productive learning moments.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading once, download it, and then spend days working through it offline. The LMS would show one quick login, but I might have spent six or seven hours with that document over the week. It was more efficient for me to have everything saved locally where I could organize it my way.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became incredibly hard. I was scared constantly, worried I'd fail or fall too far behind to recover. The workload felt crushing, and my stress levels were through the roof. There were days I'd just stare at my laptop unable to start anything because the anxiety was paralyzing. That's when the peer support really helped me push through.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just click around without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities, like part-time work and personal obligations, meant I sometimes had to choose between sleeping and studying. The fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully even when I did have time to log in.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open readings or scroll through modules feeling completely checked out, my mind racing with everything else I needed to do. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but mentally I was somewhere else entirely. Those sessions felt like going through the motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The fear of doing them poorly made starting feel impossible sometimes. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, but really I was just avoiding the anxiety of potentially failing. By the time I finally started, I'd rush through and not produce my best work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have intense bursts of LMS activity right before something was due, cramming and uploading at the last minute. Without deadlines, I probably would have just kept reviewing and never felt ready enough to submit. They were stressful but necessary for me to actually complete things.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with that particular course or topic. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that area entirely, at least temporarily. The timing mattered too, if feedback came quickly, I could adjust, but delayed feedback often came after I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The structure helped in some ways because it gave me a roadmap, but having seventeen courses meant the structures often conflicted with each other. When modules were clearly laid out with explicit expectations, I could plan better. Courses with vague structures made my anxiety worse because I never knew if I was doing enough.
Q19: Clear weekly checklists made engagement much easier because I could see exactly what needed doing. What made it harder was when important information was buried in multiple clicks or when different courses organized things inconsistently. Having to hunt for materials when I was already stressed added unnecessary friction to an already difficult process.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week, clicking around trying to figure out what to do but being too overwhelmed to actually complete anything. High activity might mean someone is lost or anxious, searching for clarity rather than productively working. It could also be someone who engages deeply with learning but struggles with the final output.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was decent despite my low LMS engagement because I did most of my real studying offline. The LMS doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes or discussing concepts with peers in person. Someone can learn effectively through methods that don't leave digital footprints.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in and what they click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or consistency. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, whether someone actually understood what they accessed. They also miss offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone was genuinely learning or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious enough to dig deeper, when concepts start connecting in my mind, and when I feel invested in my own learning. It's not about how many times I log in but whether I'm mentally present and growing from the experience.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how the peer support group literally kept me going when I wanted to give up. It wouldn't capture the late nights rewriting notes by hand, the relief when a concept finally clicked, or the emotional toll of managing so many courses at once. My engagement was as much emotional survival as it was academic work.
